topic: Misc - Rust Nightly Features in Libraries

turns.content: Hey, I just implemented a function using an unstable feature from the nightly channel. It works great! But is there a better way to do it?

That's good you're asking! Sometimes relying on nightly features can introduce maintenance headaches. Have you considered checking if the feature is stabilized yet? You could use `cfg` attributes or `feature-gates` in your Cargo.toml to conditionally compile based on whether the feature is stable.
Yeah, that's what I thought. But how do I know which features are stable and which aren't?

You can check rust-lang.org for the current stability status of each feature. Alternatively, you can use `rustup component list` to see available components, including experimental ones like `rust-src`, which lets you access compiler internals needed for some nightly-only APIs.
Okay, so I should avoid nightly features unless necessary? And if I must use them, it's better to have fallback code?

Exactly! Always prefer stable APIs. If you need experimental stuff but want your crate to compile on stable Rust too, you can write a thin wrapper around nightly-only calls and provide stub implementations for stable channels. This way, users get the latest features when available, but your package remains usable everywhere else.

topic: Misc - Rust Nightly Features in Libraries

turns.content: I'm reviewing a library crate I wrote earlier and noticed it uses some nightly APIs. My assumption is that since the code compiles on nightly, I can just add `#![feature(...)]` to the top of every file in the crate. Is this actually how we handle unstable features?

That's a common beginner mistake; you don't need to annotate every single file. You only add the feature gate at the very top of `lib.rs` once if the crate is pure Rust, or in each module where that specific nightly syntax appears if it's mixed with legacy code. Think of it like a master key: adding one gate to the main door lets you enter the whole building without needing keys for every individual room.

So I can just put `#![feature(allocator_api)]` at the top of `lib.rs` and it applies everywhere. But what if some modules are already stabilized in our internal library? Do we still need to gate them?

Exactly right for pure Rust, but you only gate the parts that actually use the nightly feature. If a module has no unstable code, it doesn't need the gate; the compiler will warn if you try to use an unstable thing without gating it in its own file anyway. It's like wearing sunglasses: you don't wear them on your whole body just because they're cool, only where you need light protection.
I'm confused about documentation too. Should I document the nightly feature in the crate docs? And what if we plan to stabilize it internally but not yet release a public version?

Yes, you must document any unstable API usage clearly so downstream users know not to depend on it. Even if you intend to stabilize it later, the docs should state that this is nightly-only and explain when the feature will be stabilized internally or potentially dropped. Treat it like a temporary patch in your system: note exactly what's broken and how long until it's fixed.
What about trait implementations? If I implement `impl core::fmt::Debug` using an unstable feature, do I still need the gate on that impl block?

Yes, every place where you use a nightly-only API needs its own gate unless the whole crate is gated. So your `impl core::fmt::Debug` for some type will need a feature flag if it uses an unstable helper function. It's like requiring a special badge to enter any room that has a new door.
I'm also worried about CI failures. If we gate features, won't our nightly-only builds fail if someone changes the feature list? Should we pin the rust version?

You should always pin your build environment to a specific nightly or use `RUSTFLAGS` to ensure consistent gates. Also, consider using cargo-features or similar tools so that feature gates are managed consistently across your team's CI pipelines.
What if we want to expose an unstable API in our public crate but only for internal use? How do we document that distinction?
You can add a `#[doc(hidden)]` or create a separate module with a clear warning comment. The key is to make it obvious in the docs that this API is unstable and not meant for external consumption.
One last thing: what happens if we accidentally use an unstable feature without gating, but it doesn't break compilation? Should we still fix it?

Absolutely. Even if the compiler lets you compile, relying on unstabilized APIs is risky and violates soundness principles. You should always gate unstable features to ensure predictability across different Rust versions.

topic: Misc - Rust Nightly Features in Libraries

turns.content: I'm trying to use a nightly-only feature from the std library in my crate, but I'm getting an error saying the symbol is not available. The documentation says this should work with `#![feature]`, so why am I failing?

It sounds like you might be missing the attribute gate on your crate root. For nightly features exposed to external crates, you need to add `#![feature(whatever_feature)]` at the very top of lib.rs before any other attributes or imports. Without this line, the compiler treats it as if you're running stable Rust and rejects unknown symbols immediately.

Don't lose your frustration; this is exactly why Rust has explicit gatekeeping. In Java, you might just import a class and it works if the version matches, but in Rust, every nightly feature requires an explicit `#![feature(...)]` on the crate or module to be visible. The compiler enforces this strictly to prevent accidental use of unstable code that could break future versions.
Okay, I get that, but what happens if a library author wants to expose such features? Do they just leave it un gated and let users figure it out?

No, reputable library authors will explicitly document the nightly requirement in their README and changelog. They might also provide a `nightly` feature flag within their own crate using cfg-attributes like `#[cfg(feature = "nightly")]` to conditionally compile code only when users enable that flag at build time. This gives them control over who gets the experimental functionality.
So if I'm building a library myself and want to use a nightly feature, do I just assume everyone will run on nightly?

That's a dangerous assumption you should avoid. Most users run stable Rust by default. You should either require the user to be on nightly in your dependencies (which often breaks CI for non-nightly runners) or provide an optional nightly feature flag that only activates when explicitly requested, ensuring stability for the majority of users.
I see, but what about documentation? If I document a nightly feature without warning, will people just use it and then get confused later?

Exactly. That is why Rust requires clear warnings in your docs when you expose unstable APIs. You must explicitly state that the API is experimental and may change between versions. Without this warning, users might rely on an implementation detail that gets removed when the feature stabilizes or changes its behavior.
This makes sense, but I'm still stuck on why my local build works fine for me but fails in CI. Is it possible the nightly channel is different?

Yes, that's a very common gotcha. The Rust compiler version can vary slightly between your local machine and the CI runner depending on how you run `rustup update` or if you're using a specific toolchain profile. Ensure both environments are explicitly pinned to the same nightly version using rust-toolchain files with explicit channel names like 'nightly' rather than relying on defaults.
